title: Processes, Signals, Jobs, and Resource Control · Operations Case: Shell Job Control
summary: Diagnose a worker that appears hung while preserving evidence, avoiding collateral termination, and distinguishing shell jobs from system processes. Planned artifact: Add and verify the linux shell job control increment in a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Use foreground, background, jobs, fg, bg, wait, disown, and nohup with terminal and session behavior in mind.
Send signals from least disruptive to forceful while accounting for handlers, permissions, descendants, and cleanup.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# linux-basics cumulative operations evidence notebook
# Commands run only in the deterministic stateful terminal simulator.
# Record hypothesis, command, expected signal, observation, conclusion, and recovery.

steps.title: Predict Shell Job Control
steps.instruction:
theory case — Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2204; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Shell Job Control contract for the supplied data.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Capture identity, path, process, file, and service state; predict parsing and scope; run one safe simulated observation; interpret exit status; then change one bounded layer and verify rollback.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Process tree has shell 2100, worker 2201, and child 2204; the worker stops responding but owns a temporary report. Identify the intended process, capture state, send the least disruptive signal, and verify cleanup. A defensible solution applies Shell Job Control, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to a process-tree incident and controlled-recovery runbook.

content.code:
pgrep -a -P 2100
ps -o pid,ppid,stat,ni,cmd -p 2201
kill -TERM 2201
# Verify exit and cleanup before considering a stronger signal.
content.caption: Runnable safe terminal evidence example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.
